---
title: "Online Supplement for CRPTO: Auditing Temporal Transport and Comparator Choice in Conformal Portfolios"
author: "Anonymous"
lang: en
bibliography: ../book/references.bib
csl: ../book/apa.csl
link-citations: true
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: false
    css: ijds.css
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    number-sections: false
    geometry:
      - margin=0.85in
execute:
  freeze: auto
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

# Scope and Evidence Contract

This supplement supports one retrospective CRPTO audit. It does not describe
successive paper variants or select a policy after observing outcomes. All nine
combinations of risk tolerance and conformal mixing weight are co-primary. The
audited objects are fixed score taxonomies, two availability-safe residual
windows, monthly allocations, three comparator rules, scoped point-cap
frontiers, sharp bounds for unresolved binary outcomes, and a controlled
simulation. The late timing protocol was motivated by the early audit and was
locked before its own outcome evaluation. It is a design sensitivity, not a
result-based replacement for the reference window.

The archive had been inspected in earlier project work. The current protocol
was locked before its complete run, but it is not a pristine lockbox and not a
prospective trial or preregistration. Exact repository coordinates are omitted
from anonymous-review surfaces and preserved in an editor-only crosswalk.

| Reader question | Location | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| Can every candidate exist at decision time? | Appendix A | Yes; membership is status independent |
| What receives the 90% target? | Appendix B | A binary-outcome conformal interval on candidate rows |
| Is one guardrail selected? | Appendix C | No; all nine policies are co-primary |
| What is exact? | Appendix D | Score-cap algebra and unrestricted-outcome bounds |
| Which empirical signs survive? | Appendix E | Candidate coverage failure; no portfolio sign over any declared comparator scope |
| What drives the score effect? | Appendix F | Fixed stratum penalties more than endpoint clipping |
| Can the evidence be replayed? | Appendix G | Yes; frozen allocations and evaluation have explicit lineage |
| What may the paper claim? | Appendix H | Audit findings, not superiority, causality, or selected-set validity |

: Reader map for the Online Supplement. {#tbl-s-reader-map}

# Appendix A: Data and Outcome Observability

## A.1 Source and chronology

The source is the Lending Club 2007--2020Q3 research snapshot. It contains
2,925,493 rows, of which 2,060,077 have 36-month terms. Deterministic date,
schema, and origination-feature checks retain 540,121 rows in the early design
and 625,576 in the late design. The common OOT panel has 465,117 loans. The
unit of decision is an issue month, and each of the 15 primary months receives
a new USD 1 million budget.

| Block | Issue dates | Rows | Labels available/read | Role |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| PD development | 2007-06--2010-12 | 17,433 | 17,392 | model fit and temporal validation |
| Probability calibration | 2011-01--2011-12 | 14,101 | 14,077 | Platt fit and taxonomy edges |
| Early residual fit | 2012-01--2012-06 | 14,967 | 14,948 | reference recipe |
| Early policy development | 2012-07--2012-12 | 28,503 | not read | reference fixed comparator |
| Late residual fit | 2012-07--2013-01 | 34,040 | 33,909 | timing sensitivity recipe |
| Late policy development | 2013-02--2013-12 | 94,885 | not read | timing-sensitivity comparator |
| Primary OOT | 2016-04--2017-06 | 376,890 | post-freeze only | co-primary evaluation |
| Censored extension | 2017-07--2017-09 | 88,227 | post-freeze only | censoring stress |

: S1 chronology and label observability. Early and late rows overlap and must not be summed. {#tbl-s-chronology}

## A.2 Endpoint taxonomy

Statuses containing `Charged Off` are positive and statuses containing `Fully
Paid` are negative. Exact `Default` and all nonterminal states are unresolved:
the administrative snapshot does not prove the protocol's terminal charged-off
event. Unresolved rows remain in candidate menus and are bounded only after
allocations have been frozen. The endpoint is a terminal snapshot
classification, not lifetime default risk, a hazard, or a funding response.

For label-dependent fitting, a Fully Paid label becomes observable at its last
payment date. A Charged Off label is conservatively available six months after
last payment. With the March 31, 2016 information cutoff, the retained fitting
shares are 0.997648 for PD development, 0.998298 for probability calibration,
0.998731 for the early residual fit, and 0.996152 for the late residual fit.

## A.3 Physical information boundary

The decision panel contains only ID, issue month, amount, purpose, contractual
rate, Platt-scaled default score, conformal endpoints, and fixed score group. It rejects
status, realized payoff, miscoverage, or any outcome-derived field. The outcome
panel is keyed separately. A validated one-to-one join occurs after every
allocation is persisted; missing or duplicate IDs fail the run. These checks
make leakage auditable but do not create exchangeability.

# Appendix B: Prediction and Conformal Construction

## B.1 Platt-scaled default score

A CatBoost model uses 29 numeric and 9 categorical origination-time features.
Its fixed settings are 500 trees, depth 6, learning rate 0.04, class balancing,
Bernoulli subsampling, and temporal ordering. The final 20% of development
months form a validation tail. A logistic Platt map is fitted on 2011 margins.
Seeds 40--44 are a declared sensitivity; no OOT metric chooses among them.

| Role | Candidates | Resolved | Default rate | AUC | Brier | Log loss | ECE (10 bins) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| PD validation | 3,835 | 3,835 | 0.100391 | 0.688986 | 0.139581 | 0.441863 | 0.205118 |
| Platt calibration | 14,077 | 14,077 | 0.104781 | 0.676327 | 0.090206 | 0.316543 | 0.003273 |
| Late policy development | 94,885 | 94,885 | 0.123117 | 0.643544 | 0.105093 | 0.359119 | 0.003330 |
| Primary OOT | 376,890 | 365,339 | 0.157380 | 0.640848 | 0.130753 | 0.430859 | 0.048323 |
| Censored extension | 88,227 | 59,502 | 0.199287 | 0.652685 | 0.161378 | 0.513924 | 0.091447 |

: S2 resolved-row prediction diagnostics for canonical seed 42. ECE is equal-width ten-bin expected calibration error; unresolved rows do not enter AUC, Brier, log loss, or ECE. {#tbl-s-prediction-diagnostics}

## B.2 Availability-safe split Mondrian interval

For Platt-scaled score $p_i$ and binary endpoint $Y_i$, the residual is
$s_i=|Y_i-p_i|$. Group boundaries are quantiles of all 2011 scores; they do not
use either residual-window label. Within a frozen group $g$ with $n_g$
observable labels, the residual radius is the order statistic at

$$
k_g=\left\lceil(n_g+1)(1-0.10)\right\rceil.
$$

The future clipped residual interval is

$$
[\ell_i,u_i]=[\max(0,p_i-c_{g(i)}),\min(1,p_i+c_{g(i)})].
$$

The seed-42 five-group boundaries are 0.008219, 0.050265, 0.080369,
0.111894, 0.153262, and 0.475854. Pooled, two-group, and ten-group recipes are
coarsening diagnostics fixed by the same rule. The interval predicts the
binary outcome in the ambient real line. Its endpoint $u_i$ is used as a
decision score; it is neither a confidence limit for latent PD nor the convex
hull of a discrete prediction set.

| Window | Groups | Fit coverage | Primary resolved coverage | All-candidate bound | Mean width | $u=1$ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Early | 1 | 0.900388 | 0.876542 | [0.853029, 0.880326] | 0.924935 | 0.124901 |
| Early | 2 | 0.900388 | 0.878403 | [0.855929, 0.881820] | 0.583563 | 0.164961 |
| Early | 5 | 0.900388 | 0.876312 | [0.854714, 0.879647] | 0.664992 | 0.187084 |
| Early | 10 | 0.900388 | 0.878614 | [0.857404, 0.881942] | 0.666072 | 0.203415 |
| Late | 1 | 0.900174 | 0.871465 | [0.847518, 0.875404] | 0.917102 | 0.104123 |
| Late | 2 | 0.900174 | 0.871147 | [0.848279, 0.874730] | 0.580530 | 0.141304 |
| Late | 5 | 0.900174 | 0.867452 | [0.845072, 0.870973] | 0.663749 | 0.152198 |
| Late | 10 | 0.900174 | 0.868714 | [0.846860, 0.872215] | 0.581416 | 0.170137 |

: S3 primary coverage under two residual windows and score taxonomies fixed before OOT. Each all-candidate bound uses 376,890 rows, including 11,551 unresolved outcomes. {#tbl-s-coverage}

All eight upper endpoints are below 0.90. The empirical failure is temporal and
precedes portfolio selection; it is not a contradiction of finite-sample
validity under exchangeability.

## B.3 Charged-off reporting-lag sensitivity

The late window is recomputed on a closed lag grid. No policy is reoptimized;
the exercise changes only which residual labels are observable by the fixed
information cutoff and the resulting residual order statistics.

| Lag (months) | Fit rows | Fit coverage | Primary resolved coverage | All-candidate bound | Mean width | $u=1$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | 33,978 | 0.900200 | 0.869267 | [0.847024, 0.872767] | 0.664004 | 0.158197 |
| 3 | 33,971 | 0.900209 | 0.869130 | [0.846849, 0.872634] | 0.663975 | 0.157346 |
| 6 | 33,909 | 0.900174 | 0.867452 | [0.845072, 0.870973] | 0.663749 | 0.152198 |
| 12 | 33,448 | 0.900173 | 0.858066 | [0.835215, 0.861705] | 0.499547 | 0.125063 |

: S4 five-stratum late-window label-lag sensitivity. Coverage denominators are candidate rows, and unresolved outcomes receive sharp worst/best completion. {#tbl-s-label-lags}

All four upper endpoints remain below 0.90. The geometry is not smooth: at 12
months, one group residual quantile crosses a binary order-statistic threshold,
reducing width materially despite excluding only 461 additional fit rows.

# Appendix C: Portfolio and Comparator Protocol

## C.1 Coherent payoff

For annual contractual rate $r_i$ and loss given default $\lambda$, the
standardized realized payoff and model-implied plug-in objective are

$$
\pi_i(Y_i)=(1-Y_i)r_i-Y_i\lambda,
\qquad
\bar\pi_i=(1-p_i)r_i-p_i\lambda.
$$

The plug-in coefficient is a conditional expectation only if $p_i$ equals the
conditional default probability. The canonical $\lambda$ is 0.45; 0.25 and
0.65 are sensitivities. This endpoint omits timing, amortization, prepayment,
recoveries, fees, and discounting. An undiscounted snapshot cash-yield
diagnostic is secondary and is not called IRR or investor return.

## C.2 Monthly program and closed policy family

Each policy maximizes $\sum_i a_i\bar\pi_i$ subject to a full USD 1 million
monthly budget, loan amount bounds, a weighted risk-score cap, and a purpose
concentration cap. The guardrail score is
$q_i=(1-\gamma)p_i+\gamma u_i$.

| Policy | $\tau$ | $\gamma$ |
|---|---:|---:|
| linear-001 | 0.15 | 0.25 |
| linear-002 | 0.15 | 0.50 |
| linear-003 | 0.15 | 0.75 |
| linear-004 | 0.17 | 0.25 |
| linear-005 | 0.17 | 0.50 |
| linear-006 | 0.17 | 0.75 |
| linear-007 | 0.19 | 0.25 |
| linear-008 | 0.19 | 0.50 |
| linear-009 | 0.19 | 0.75 |

: S5 complete co-primary policy family. IDs are labels, not ranks. {#tbl-s-policy-grid}

## C.3 Comparator rules

`C0` copies the guardrail's numeric cap onto the point score. `C1` fixes, before OOT,
the guardrail's mean funded point-score moment over the development allocations
following each residual window (six early, eleven late).
`C2` freezes a guardrail allocation, computes its monthly funded point-score
moment, and uses that value as the point-score cap on the identical menu. C2
matches 135 canonical policy-month moments with maximum absolute residual
$4.17\times10^{-17}$; it uses no outcomes but is an audit comparator rather
than a deployable fixed rule.

Comparator envelopes are reported for three scopes: the three core rules;
those rules plus nine development-supported point caps from 0.0600 to 0.0825;
and a broad stress scope with 29 point caps from 0.05 to 0.12. Canonical
settings are seed 42, purpose cap 0.25, and LGD 0.45. Sensitivities cross five
seeds, purpose caps 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, and 1.00, three LGDs, and four taxonomies.
Every cell is retained.

# Appendix D: Exact Statements and Bounds

## D.1 Positive affine score equivalence

If $s_i=ap_i+b$, $a>0$, and the budget binds at $B$, then

$$
\sum_i a_i s_i\le \tau_sB
\Longleftrightarrow
\sum_i a_i p_i\le ((\tau_s-b)/a)B.
$$

The proof substitutes the affine score and uses $\sum_i a_i=B$. A pooled
affine placebo reproduces the translated point-score allocation to solver
tolerance. The actual guardrail
$q_i=p_i+\gamma\min\{c_{g(i)},1-p_i\}$ is piecewise affine and
stratum-specific, so it does not generally admit one global cap translation.

## D.2 Same-cap nesting

Because $u_i\ge p_i$ and $\gamma\in[0,1]$, $q_i\ge p_i$. Hence every
guardrail-feasible allocation at cap $\tau$ is point-score feasible at the same
cap. For the common maximization objective, the C0 point optimum cannot be
worse in expected objective. This is a positive control, not a neutral
comparison of uncertainty methods.

## D.3 Binary miscoverage

**Identity S1 (binary interval geometry).**
For $Y_i\in\{0,1\}$,

$$
m_i=\mathbf 1\{Y_i=0,\ell_i>0\}+
\mathbf 1\{Y_i=1,u_i<1\}.
$$

The two terms are disjoint and follow by enumerating $Y=0$ and $Y=1$.
Endpoint saturation can therefore raise coverage while adding little ranking
information.

## D.4 Sharp unrestricted-outcome bounds

For a fixed allocation and additive metric
$T(Y)=C+\sum_{i\in U}g_i(Y_i)$ over unresolved rows $U$, the bounds

$$
T_L=C+\sum_{i\in U}\min_{y\in\{0,1\}}g_i(y),\qquad
T_U=C+\sum_{i\in U}\max_{y\in\{0,1\}}g_i(y)
$$

are sharp because joint endpoint assignments attain them. Paired policy bounds
use the union of funded IDs and optimize each shared unresolved outcome once;
subtracting marginal intervals would generally be wider and not sharp.

## D.5 Selection-transport accounting identity

For any resolved metric, let $T_r$ be its candidate-row mean, $T_e$ its
available-exposure-weighted candidate mean, $T_g$ the counterfactual obtained by
combining candidate within-group means with funded group shares, and $T_f$ its
funded-exposure-weighted mean. Relative to reference $T_0$,

$$
T_f-T_0=(T_r-T_0)+(T_e-T_r)+(T_g-T_e)+(T_f-T_g).
$$

The terms respectively describe row-to-reference, available-exposure
weighting, funded group composition, and within-group selection. This is an
accounting identity, not an orthogonal causal decomposition. With unresolved
outcomes it can be evaluated under each extremal completion, but intermediate
terms are completion-specific; only the final fixed-allocation endpoints are
claimed sharp.

## D.6 Declared comparator sensitivity envelope

For a finite comparator set $\mathcal J$, the envelope
$[\min_jL_j,\max_jU_j]$ has an invariant sign exactly when it lies strictly on
one side of zero. This is a deterministic summary over named design choices,
not an identified set for a latent causal effect or a sampling confidence
interval.

# Appendix E: Empirical Comparator Audit

## E.1 Canonical timing and comparator census

| Window/comparator | Payoff $-$ / $+$ / ? | Default $-$ / $+$ / ? | Miscoverage $-$ / $+$ / ? |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Early C0 same cap | 0 / 9 / 0 | 9 / 0 / 0 | 8 / 0 / 1 |
| Late C0 same cap | 0 / 9 / 0 | 9 / 0 / 0 | 7 / 0 / 2 |
| Early C1 fixed development | 5 / 2 / 2 | 2 / 2 / 5 | 1 / 7 / 1 |
| Late C1 fixed development | 5 / 0 / 4 | 0 / 5 / 4 | 0 / 8 / 1 |
| Early C2 contemporaneous | 7 / 0 / 2 | 0 / 1 / 8 | 0 / 8 / 1 |
| Late C2 contemporaneous | 5 / 0 / 4 | 0 / 1 / 8 | 0 / 8 / 1 |

: S6 guardrail-minus-point direction counts in both residual windows. A question mark means the sharp common-outcome interval contains zero. {#tbl-s-direction-census}

C0 uses a weaker point-score constraint. Nesting orders the optimized plug-in
objective, not realized payoff or default; the uniform C0 realized directions
are empirical and must not be attributed to the nesting result. Under exact C2
matching, the early and late payoff counts differ, while neither window yields
a universal default or miscoverage direction. Specifically, payoff is lower
for 7 of 9 early guardrails and 5 of 9 late guardrails. No timing window is
promoted.

## E.2 Canonical C2 sharp contrasts

| Policy | Payoff bound ($) | Default bound | Miscoverage bound |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| linear-001 | [-7,380, 89,594] | [-0.009346, 0.000347] | [-0.001730, 0.006762] |
| linear-002 | [-170,958, -23,356] | [-0.001677, 0.013084] | [0.007470, 0.020645] |
| linear-003 | [-195,812, -26,691] | [-0.002158, 0.015087] | [0.012104, 0.027095] |
| linear-004 | [-45,995, 7,034] | [-0.001518, 0.003816] | [0.001487, 0.005621] |
| linear-005 | [-135,905, -6,936] | [-0.003170, 0.009673] | [0.006988, 0.018245] |
| linear-006 | [-182,723, -30,427] | [-0.001139, 0.014268] | [0.011468, 0.025099] |
| linear-007 | [-43,303, -3,211] | [-0.000845, 0.003008] | [0.000033, 0.003102] |
| linear-008 | [-157,070, -25,322] | [-0.000519, 0.012567] | [0.007330, 0.018829] |
| linear-009 | [-218,919, -74,358] | [0.002888, 0.017311] | [0.013610, 0.026446] |

: S7 early-window canonical C2 paired sharp bounds, seed 42, purpose cap 0.25, LGD 0.45. {#tbl-s-c2-bounds}

These are retrospective fixed-policy differences, not treatment effects. The
only policy with a sign-robust default increase is linear-009. It is not
promoted as a representative winner or loser.

| Policy | Late payoff bound ($) | Late default bound | Late miscoverage bound |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| linear-001 | [-15,407, 82,916] | [-0.008628, 0.001203] | [-0.001601, 0.007031] |
| linear-002 | [-168,704, -21,204] | [-0.001907, 0.012846] | [0.004446, 0.017612] |
| linear-003 | [-203,853, -34,732] | [-0.001382, 0.015863] | [0.012250, 0.027242] |
| linear-004 | [-47,188, 4,953] | [-0.001305, 0.003944] | [0.000923, 0.004972] |
| linear-005 | [-125,832, 2,708] | [-0.004020, 0.008778] | [0.002628, 0.013839] |
| linear-006 | [-184,086, -27,619] | [-0.001331, 0.014530] | [0.009153, 0.022760] |
| linear-007 | [-38,912, 1,180] | [-0.001270, 0.002583] | [0.000030, 0.003883] |
| linear-008 | [-162,499, -28,901] | [-0.000129, 0.013136] | [0.005970, 0.017648] |
| linear-009 | [-219,372, -74,811] | [0.002916, 0.017339] | [0.010614, 0.023451] |

: S8 late-window canonical C2 paired sharp bounds. The design and sign orientation match S7. {#tbl-s-late-c2-bounds}

## E.3 Point-cap frontier

At point caps 0.05--0.065, at least eight guardrails have lower payoff, while
six to nine have higher default and all nine have higher miscoverage. At cap
0.0825, all nine have higher payoff and lower default, and eight have lower
miscoverage. At caps 0.085--0.095, all nine directions are favorable on all
three metrics except one payoff interval at 0.0875 and 0.09. The frontier then
becomes less uniform at selected higher caps. This pattern is why one cap
cannot stand in for comparator sensitivity.

For the late timing design, the outcome-free development targets range from
0.060040 to 0.081499; rounding outward on the fixed 0.0025 grid gives the
development-supported scope 0.0600--0.0825. Every policy-metric envelope crosses
zero even before adding the broad stress tails.

| Comparator scope | Records per policy | Payoff ? | Default ? | Miscoverage ? |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Core C0--C2 | 3 | 9 / 9 | 9 / 9 | 9 / 9 |
| Development-supported | 12 | 9 / 9 | 9 / 9 | 9 / 9 |
| Broad stress | 32 | 9 / 9 | 9 / 9 | 9 / 9 |

: S9 indeterminate policy counts by metric and comparator scope. Nine of nine means all sharp envelopes contain zero. The machine-readable evidence reports all 81 policy-level endpoints. {#tbl-s-comparator-scopes}

## E.4 Seeds and purpose caps

Purpose caps 0.20, 0.25, and 0.30 bind in all 2,025 reference
guardrail-month cells; cap 1.00 removes that constraint. Each residual window
has 180 C2 seed-purpose cells:

| Window | Metric | Negative | Positive | Indeterminate |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Early | Standardized payoff | 59 | 37 | 84 |
| Early | Weighted default | 50 | 33 | 97 |
| Early | Weighted miscoverage | 33 | 64 | 83 |
| Late | Standardized payoff | 56 | 36 | 88 |
| Late | Weighted default | 51 | 33 | 96 |
| Late | Weighted miscoverage | 27 | 85 | 68 |

: S10 complete C2 seed-purpose census in both timing designs. {#tbl-s-seed-cap}

The full machine-readable table reports every seed-policy-cap cell. Mixed signs
show interaction between score perturbations and binding operational
constraints, not Monte Carlo uncertainty around one selected policy.

## E.5 LGD and cash endpoint

Reoptimization at LGD 0.25, 0.45, and 0.65 changes multiple payoff and default
directions. The canonical LGD is a declared normalization, not an estimated
recovery model. The secondary snapshot cash-yield diagnostic agrees with the
canonical C2 payoff direction for six policies and differs for three. Economic
claims are therefore restricted to the explicitly defined endpoint.

# Appendix F: Mechanism and Controlled Simulation

## F.1 Frozen-score ablations

The five-group score contains fixed stratum penalties and clipping at one.
Removing clipping while retaining the penalties makes comparatively small
allocation changes: USD 4,685--46,609 across policies. Replacing group
penalties with one pooled affine penalty changes USD 9.849--21.352 million of
the USD 15 million canonical allocation. The pooled score with its translated
cap reproduces the point score exactly. These distances are recomputed from the
frozen exposure vectors. The material channel is the interaction of
stratum-specific penalties with binding constraints, not the conformal label
itself.

## F.2 Simulation design and result

At each temporal shift in $\{0,0.05,0.10,0.15\}$, 200 repetitions independently
generate calibration and evaluation samples, fit the same binary residual
construction, and solve same-cap and exactly matched point comparators.

| Shift | Calibration coverage | Transported coverage | Saturation | Taxonomy allocation $L_1$ | Guardrail $-$ C0 default | Guardrail $-$ C2 default |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0.00 | 0.903340 | 0.901618 | 0.094375 | 0.052318 | -0.069322 | -0.002634 |
| 0.05 | 0.903375 | 0.898707 | 0.105928 | 0.056200 | -0.072641 | 0.001092 |
| 0.10 | 0.903385 | 0.895058 | 0.112745 | 0.055684 | -0.076036 | 0.000132 |
| 0.15 | 0.903380 | 0.892312 | 0.123423 | 0.057688 | -0.081602 | -0.002178 |

: S11 simulation means over 200 repetitions per shift. {#tbl-s-simulation}

The 5--95% intervals for guardrail-minus-C2 default contain zero at every
shift. Clipping-only allocation distance remains below 0.002 on average, while
taxonomy distance remains 0.052--0.058. The simulation is a mechanism check,
not a calibrated model of Lending Club.

# Appendix G: Reproducibility and Lineage

## G.1 Outcome-free freezes and timing sensitivity

The first locked execution completed every outcome-free operation: 493,620
decision rows, five fitted models, four taxonomies, 7,347 solve records, and
718,925 funded allocation rows. It wrote a cryptographic freeze before any
outcome join. Its original evaluator repeatedly merged the full outcome panel
and did not finish within the execution window.

The recovery execution validates the source protocol, model and recipe
descriptors, row cardinalities, and allocation hashes, then reuses only frozen
outcome-free objects. One vectorized join produces 7,347 monthly evaluations,
515 aggregate rows, 504 paired contrasts, 594 coverage rows, and 800 simulation
repetitions. It changes evaluation complexity, not scores or allocations.

The late timing run is independent rather than a continuation of the recovery
evaluator. It materializes 560,002 decision rows, five residual recipes, 7,437
solve records, and 729,789 funded rows before its outcome join. Evaluation
produces 7,437 monthly records, 515 aggregates, 504 paired contrasts, 704
coverage rows, and 768 label-lag rows. All solves are optimal under native
HiGHS, all budgets bind, and the maximum C2 moment residual is
$4.17\times10^{-17}$.

The point path provides a code-equivalence control. Early and late runs share
exactly 465,117 OOT loans; their point scores have maximum absolute difference
zero. Across C0 and the 29-cap point frontier, all 570 canonical policy-month
cells also have zero maximum and zero total-$L_1$ exposure difference. Thus
timing differences are attributable to the residual recipe and dependent
comparators, not a hidden PD or objective implementation change.

## G.2 Evidence build and gates

The evidence builder rejects a mismatched protocol, incomplete budget,
unexpected solver, failed C2 match, missing receipt, or changed source hash. It
generates 62 table and figure files plus one manifest. Consecutive builds are
byte-identical. The review pipeline then checks claims across the QMD body,
supplement, official IJDS TeX, and generated evidence; runs unit, typing, lint,
champion-integrity, and numerical-drift gates; compiles PDFs; and inspects their
rendered pages.

The frozen run summary also retains an operational status diagnostic that
classified the literal status `Default` as resolved: 500,019 resolved and
40,102 unresolved rows. The terminal endpoint used by every coverage and
contrast calculation conservatively reclassifies those 174 rows as
unresolved, yielding 499,845 resolved and 40,276 unresolved. The evidence
builder now derives and asserts both inventories so the receipt distinction
cannot be mistaken for an endpoint change.

The protected historical champion and extraction manifest are separate legacy
objects. Neither is executed or overwritten by this audit.

## G.3 Anonymous review boundary

Run names, commits, tags, hashes, remote URLs, and machine paths are absent from
reviewer-facing files because they can reveal authorship. An editor-only
crosswalk maps the anonymous descriptions to immutable artifacts. On
acceptance, the code, environment lock, evidence manifest, content-addressed
data pointers, and raw reconstruction instructions can be released.

# Appendix H: Claim Boundary and Limitations

| Evidence | Supported statement | Not supported |
|---|---|---|
| Two windows and four fixed taxonomies | OOT candidate coverage upper bounds are below 0.90 | General conformal invalidity |
| Four late-window label lags | The coverage conclusion survives the closed lag grid | A resolution-time model or smooth interval geometry |
| C0 nesting | Same numeric cap is a weaker point-score constraint | Neutral uncertainty comparison |
| Exact C2 matching | One funded point-score moment is equal | Equal feasible sets or unique counterfactual |
| Sharp paired bounds | Fixed-policy signs under unrestricted unresolved labels | Sampling confidence or missing-at-random identification |
| 27 envelopes in each of three scopes | No sign survives core, development-supported, or broad choices | Invariance over every possible comparator |
| 360 seed-cap cells across two windows | Operational and timing choices interact with policy direction | A selected best window, seed, cap, or guardrail |
| Simulation | Drift and comparator mechanics can reproduce the pattern | Structural model of the lending market |

: S12 active claim boundary. {#tbl-s-claims}

The remaining limitations are substantive. The archive is one discontinued
retail platform; terminal status is not a survival outcome; snapshot payoff is
not discounted return; temporal exchangeability fails; funded-set coverage is
not guaranteed; C2 is an audit comparator; continuous allocation and purpose
caps simplify operations; and earlier archive inspection prevents a pristine
confirmatory interpretation. The paper's defensible result is correspondingly
narrow: candidate-coverage failure survives the declared timing checks, while
portfolio direction is not invariant to residual timing, comparator
stringency, or binding operational constraints.

The original stop rule terminated the superiority submission when neither
9-of-9 condition survived. The audit interpretation in the paper was developed
after observing that failure. It is therefore an explicitly retrospective
secondary analysis, not a prespecified rescue criterion. The late protocol was
locked before its own outcome evaluation, but it was motivated by the early
audit and remains a sensitivity rather than a confirmatory replication.
